skill.name: git-workflow
skill.description: Guidance for Git worktrees, conventional commits, commit trailers, pull requests, and safe daily Git workflows.
skill.summary: Plan cleaner Git worktrees, commits, and pull requests with practical workflow guidance.
skill.author: Cain96

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, network, and blocker patterns. Review found no malicious intent or prompt injection; most blocker alerts are false positives from Markdown and Git terminology. The skill is legitimate, but Bash-enabled Git examples can modify working trees, branches, commits, and remotes.

medium_findings.title: Bash Git Examples Can Modify Repository State
medium_findings.description: The external command alerts are true positives for Git command examples in Markdown. They are legitimate for a Git workflow skill, but commands such as commit, push, rebase, cherry-pick, stash, bisect, and worktree cleanup can change repository state if executed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and semantically tied to Git workflow guidance. Risk is moderate because the intent is legitimate, but execution can alter branches, commits, remotes, and worktrees.

medium_findings.title: Sibling Worktree Paths Can Affect Nearby Directories
medium_findings.description: The path traversal alerts are mostly false positives for Git worktree naming examples using parent-directory paths. The pattern still needs caution because worktree add and remove commands can create or delete sibling directories, including forced removal examples.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory syntax is intentional worktree documentation, not arbitrary file access. It remains a real operational risk if a user runs cleanup commands against the wrong path.

low_findings.title: Documentation Link Is Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL alert is a false positive. The line links to the public Conventional Commits specification and does not request data or send repository content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL appears only as a Markdown reference to public documentation. No network request, credential handling, or exfiltration behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static blocker alerts do not correspond to weak cryptographic operations. The matched lines are natural language, Markdown examples, placeholders, or Git workflow text.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic API calls, hashes, ciphers, or password handling. The alerts are scanner matches against ordinary prose and formatting.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Checklist Text
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts are false positives. The matched content describes an API route, test checklist items, and a common mistakes section, with no scanning behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are documentation and checklist text, not commands that enumerate hosts, ports, users, or system configuration. No hidden intent was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Forced Worktree Removal Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents a forced worktree removal command. It is legitimate Git guidance, but it can delete uncommitted files in a selected worktree.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is directly present and has destructive semantics when applied to a dirty worktree. The surrounding text frames it as an optional cleanup action.

dangerous_patterns.title: History-Rewriting Rebase Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes interactive rebase examples. This can be appropriate before review, but it can rewrite local history and complicate shared branches.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Interactive rebase commands are shown clearly. The operational risk is real but common in Git workflow documentation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Push Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The daily checklist tells users to push regularly. This may send local commits to a configured Git remote, so users should confirm the remote and branch.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly references git push. It is expected workflow advice, but remote publication can expose unintended commits if used carelessly.

content.value_statement: Git teams often struggle with messy branches, unclear commits, and inconsistent pull requests. This skill gives structured guidance for worktrees, conventional commits, trailers, reviews, and cleanup.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to create, name, list, prune, and remove Git worktrees.
Provides conventional commit formats for features, fixes, docs, tests, builds, and chores.
Shows commit message bodies and trailers for issues, reporters, reviewers, and co-authors.
Gives pull request title, description, testing, review, and cleanup guidance.
Covers advanced Git workflows such as rebase, cherry-pick, stash, and bisect.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not validate the current repository state.
Git commands can change files, history, or remotes if the user executes them.
It does not replace project-specific branch protection or release policies.
Examples use generic branch names and must be adapted before use.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Commits
use_cases.description: Use consistent commit types, scopes, bodies, and trailers across a shared repository.

use_cases.title: Manage Parallel Work
use_cases.description: Create separate worktrees for features, fixes, experiments, and urgent production changes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Review-Ready Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Draft focused PR titles, summaries, test plans, related issues, and reviewer checklists.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a conventional commit message for these changes: [describe the change]. Include a short subject and an optional body.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Worktree
prompt_templates.prompt: Suggest a Git worktree name and setup steps for this task: [task]. Include cleanup steps after merge.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a pull request title and description for these changes: [summary]. Include test plan, breaking changes, and related issues.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Up Branch History
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this commit history: [paste summary]. Recommend whether to squash, reword, rebase, or leave it unchanged before opening a PR.

output_examples.input: A user added password reset email support and tests.
output_examples.output: The skill suggests a conventional commit with a feature type, an auth scope, a clear subject, and a body explaining the user impact.

output_examples.input: A developer needs to work on a bug fix while keeping a feature branch open.
output: Use a descriptive sibling directory name.
Remove the worktree after the branch is merged.

output_examples.input: A maintainer is preparing a PR for review.
output_examples.output: The skill produces a concise PR title, a summary, a change list, a test plan, and related issue references.

best_practices: Review every Git command before running it, especially commands that remove worktrees or rewrite history.
Keep commits atomic and use conventional commit subjects with useful bodies for complex changes.
Confirm the target branch and remote before pushing or opening a pull request.
anti_patterns: Do not run force cleanup commands against a path you have not verified.
Do not mix unrelated features, fixes, and refactors in one pull request.
Do not rewrite shared branch history without team agreement.

faq.question: Does this skill run Git commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users or agents must still choose which commands to run.

faq.question: Can it help with conventional commits?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes commit types, subject rules, body guidance, examples, and trailer patterns.

faq.question: Does it support Git worktrees?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers worktree naming, creation, listing, pruning, removal, and cleanup practices.

faq.question: Is it safe for protected repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with project policies. The examples include commands that can change history, branches, and remotes.

faq.question: Can it write pull request descriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides a PR structure with summary, changes, test plan, breaking changes, and related issues.

faq.question: Does it replace a code review?
faq.answer: No. It helps prepare workflow artifacts, but reviewers still need to inspect code behavior and security.
